schools.summary: The writer turning to describe the old covenant's worship: 'then verily the first covenant had also ordinances (dikaiomata — regulations, prescribed rites) of divine service (latreias — worship)' — the old covenant coming with its appointed rules and ceremonies of worship, an elaborate, God-given system carefully regulated by divine ordinance; 'and a worldly (kosmikon — of this world, earthly, material) sanctuary' — a material, earthly sanctuary (the tabernacle) belonging to this world, to be described (vv.2-5) so as to draw out its typical meaning over against the heavenly (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the first covenant had also ordinances of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary
the first covenant's ordinances and earthly sanctuary
the old covenant's regulated worship
the material earthly tabernacle

schools.summary: The description of the outer compartment: 'for there was a tabernacle made; the first (the first compartment, the outer room), wherein was the candlestick (the golden lampstand, giving light), and the table, and the shewbread (the bread of the Presence, the twelve loaves set before the Lord); which is called the sanctuary (hagia — the holy place)' — the first room of the tabernacle, with its sacred furniture each of symbolic meaning, the outer compartment distinguished from the inner Holy of Holies (v.3), which the priests entered daily (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: there was a tabernacle made; the first, wherein was the candlestick, and the table, and the shewbread
which is called the sanctuary
the first part of the tabernacle and its furniture
the holy place entered daily

schools.summary: The description of the second room: 'and after the second veil (the inner curtain separating the two compartments, the first being the entrance screen), the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all (hagia hagion — the Holy of Holies)' — beyond the inner veil lay the most sacred inner room, where God's presence dwelt above the mercy seat; the veil shutting off the Holy of Holies from the holy place, marking the boundary none but the high priest might cross (entered only once a year, v.7), the type of heaven itself (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: after the second veil, the tabernacle which is called the Holiest of all
the inner tabernacle, the Holiest of all
the Holy of Holies beyond the veil
the type of heaven

schools.summary: The items: 'which had the golden censer (or the golden altar of incense, with a textual/interpretive question whether kept within the Holy of Holies or the incense altar belonging to the inner service on the Day of Atonement), and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold' — the inner sanctuary's most sacred objects; 'wherein was the golden pot that had manna (the memorial of God's provision), and Aaron's rod that budded (the token of God's choice of the priesthood, Numbers 17), and the tables of the covenant (the two stone tablets of the law)' — three sacred relics within or associated with the ark, commemorating God's dealings with Israel (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: the golden censer, and the ark of the covenant overlaid round about with gold
the golden pot that had manna, and Aaron's rod that budded, and the tables of the covenant
the sacred contents of the Holy of Holies
relics of God's dealings with Israel

schools.summary: The description above the ark: 'and over it the cherubims of glory (the glorious winged figures) shadowing (overshadowing, with outspread wings) the mercyseat (hilasterion — the propitiatory, the golden lid of the ark)' — the two cherubim standing over the ark, their wings spread over the mercy seat, associated with the visible glory of God (the Shekinah) that dwelt above it, symbolizing his presence and majesty; the mercy seat being where the blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement, the place of propitiation; 'of which we cannot now speak particularly (in detail)' — the writer forbearing to expound each item, hastening to his main point about the two compartments and their meaning (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: over it the cherubims of glory shadowing the mercyseat
of which we cannot now speak particularly
the cherubim overshadowing the mercy seat
the place of propitiation

schools.summary: The routine of the holy place: 'now when these things were thus ordained (arranged, prepared), the priests went always (continually, daily) into the first tabernacle (the holy place), accomplishing the service of God (latreias — performing, carrying out the prescribed ritual worship)' — the ordinary priests entering the first compartment daily to perform the regular acts of worship (trimming the lamps, burning incense, setting out the showbread), the constant, repeated round of the old covenant's worship in the outer room (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the priests went always into the first tabernacle, accomplishing the service of God
the priests' daily service in the first tabernacle
the constant round of worship
the regular ministry of the holy place

schools.summary: The contrast with the daily service: 'but into the second (the Holy of Holies) went the high priest alone once every year' — only the high priest, and he only once a year (on the Day of Atonement), entering the inner sanctuary, the access rare and restricted; 'not without blood (always with blood)' — he could not enter the Holy of Holies without bringing the blood of sacrifice, blood being indispensable for approaching God's presence; 'which he offered for himself (the high priest being a sinner, having to atone for his own sins first), and for the errors (agnoematon — ignorances, sins of inadvertence) of the people' — atoning both for his own sins and the people's, the imperfection of a priest who must offer for himself (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood
which he offered for himself, and for the errors of the people
the high priest's yearly entrance with blood
atoning for himself and the people

schools.summary: The very arrangement of the tabernacle had a teaching purpose: 'the Holy Ghost this signifying (showing, making plain)' — the Spirit, who designed the tabernacle and its rites, intending by them to teach a spiritual lesson, the structure itself a divine sign; 'that the way into the holiest of all (the access to God's immediate presence) was not yet made manifest (opened, revealed), while as the first tabernacle was yet standing' — under the old covenant the way of full access to God not yet open (the people could not enter, and even the high priest only once a year), the very restriction showing the way was not yet free; and as long as the old order stood, the way remained closed, to be opened only in Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: The Holy Ghost this signifying, that the way into the holiest of all was not yet made manifest
while as the first tabernacle was yet standing
the Spirit teaching that the way into the holiest was not yet open
the restriction itself the sign

schools.summary: The old tabernacle and its service were 'a figure (parabole — a parable, symbol) for the time then present (the present age, the time of the old covenant, until the reformation)' — the whole system symbolic, pointing beyond itself to a coming reality; 'in which were offered both gifts and sacrifices' — the various offerings continually presented; 'that could not make him that did the service (the worshipper) perfect (teleiosai — complete, fully cleanse), as pertaining to the conscience' — the crucial deficiency, that they could not cleanse the conscience or give inward peace, dealing with ceremonial, outward purity (the flesh) but not with the guilt that troubles the soul (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: which was a figure for the time then present
gifts and sacrifices, that could not make him that did the service perfect, as pertaining to the conscience
the old offerings unable to perfect the conscience
outward purity but not inward cleansing

schools.summary: The nature of the old ordinances: 'which stood only in (consisted only of) meats and drinks (regulations about food and drink, clean and unclean), and divers washings (baptismois — various ceremonial cleansings), and carnal ordinances (dikaiomata sarkos — fleshly, external regulations)' — the old system dealing only with outward, physical, ceremonial matters, rules about the body and outward purity not reaching the heart, able to regulate the flesh but not renew the soul; 'imposed (laid on as a burden) on them until the time of reformation (diorthosis — the setting right, the new order, the time of the gospel)' — temporary, binding only until the coming of Christ who would set all things right (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: which stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings, and carnal ordinances
imposed on them until the time of reformation
the old ordinances imposed until the reformation
temporary, external rites

schools.summary: The great turn: 'but Christ being come (having appeared) an high priest of good things to come (the blessings of the new covenant, the good things now realized or yet to come)' — Christ appearing as high priest, bringing the good things the old system only foreshadowed; 'by (through) a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands (acheiropoieton — not constructed by human hands), that is to say, not of this building (not of this creation, not belonging to this material world)' — Christ ministering through the heavenly sanctuary, greater and more perfect than the earthly, not built by men (interpreters differing whether it means heaven itself or Christ's own body/humanity, but the point its heavenly not earthly nature) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

Cyril (with Henry): the 'greater and more perfect tabernacle not made with hands' is the heavenly sanctuary (some take it of Christ's own humanity, the true tent through which he ministers); not of this material creation, it surpasses the earthly tabernacle as the reality surpasses the shadow.

themes: Christ being come an high priest of good things to come
by a greater and more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands, that is to say, not of this building
Christ come as high priest through the greater heavenly tabernacle
the heavenly, not earthly, sanctuary

schools.summary: The great contrast: 'neither by (with) the blood of goats and calves (the animal sacrifices of the old covenant), but by his own blood he entered in once (hapax — once for all, not yearly) into the holy place (the heavenly Holy of Holies)' — Christ entering the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of animals but with his own blood, the infinitely superior sacrifice, and entering once for all (unlike the high priest's yearly entrance), his single entrance sufficient and final; 'having obtained (secured, found) eternal redemption (lytrosis — ransoming, deliverance) for us' — by his one entrance with his own blood securing an everlasting, complete redemption, not a yearly repeated atonement (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ, entering once with his own blood, obtained an eternal redemption — a deliverance complete and everlasting, needing no repetition, far surpassing the temporary, yearly atonement of the old covenant's animal blood.

themes: neither by the blood of goats and calves, but by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place
having obtained eternal redemption for us
Christ entering once by his own blood
obtaining eternal redemption

schools.summary: The premise of the argument from lesser to greater: 'for if the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer (the red heifer, Numbers 19) sprinkling the unclean, sanctifieth (sets apart, cleanses ceremonially) to the purifying of the flesh' — the old sacrifices and the water of purification (the ashes of the red heifer mixed with water, sprinkled on those defiled by contact with death) did have a real effect, removing ceremonial uncleanness so the person could rejoin worship; but they cleansed only outwardly, reaching the flesh, not the conscience, their efficacy real but external and limited (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the blood of animals and the ashes of the heifer purified only the flesh — an outward, typical cleansing that restored ceremonial purity but could not touch the conscience or take away sin, pointing forward to the inward cleansing of Christ's blood.

themes: the blood of bulls and of goats, and the ashes of an heifer sprinkling the unclean
sanctifieth to the purifying of the flesh
the old sacrifices cleansing only the flesh
outward, ceremonial purification

schools.summary: The a fortiori reasoning: 'how much more (by how much the superior) shall the blood of Christ' — if animal blood cleansed the flesh, how much more shall Christ's infinitely precious blood (the blood of the Son of God) cleanse the soul; he 'offered himself without spot (amomon — without blemish, the spotless victim) to God,' the sinless self-offering, voluntary and perfect; and his blood will 'purge (cleanse) your conscience from dead works (sinful, lifeless deeds) to serve the living God' — what the old sacrifices could not do, Christ's blood does, cleansing the conscience itself, removing the guilt that troubles the soul, freeing the believer to serve God. On 'the eternal Spirit': Barnes (preferring it) and Calvin take it as the Holy Spirit, under whose influences Christ offered himself (the technical sense of 'the Spirit,' the reading of most plain readers, supported by Christ's anointing, John 3:34).

Robertson and Ellicott take 'an eternal Spirit' as Christ's own divine nature, not the Holy Spirit — 'Christ's own spirit which is eternal as he is,' the higher principle of his spiritual life (a spirit of holiness, of indissoluble life, 7:16) that made his self-offering possible and gave it infinite worth; through this eternal spirit he of himself laid down his life. Grotius (noted by Calvin) takes it as 'endless life,' the eternal spirit contrasted with the perishable victims.

Wesley: 'through the eternal Spirit' — the work of redemption being the work of the whole Trinity; Christ offered himself, infinitely more precious than any created victim, without spot to God, to purge the conscience from all the inward and outward works of the devil, that we may serve the living God in faith, love, and holiness.

debates.question: What is 'the eternal Spirit' through whom Christ offered himself?

sides.position: The Holy Spirit, under whose influences Christ offered himself
Christ's own divine nature / eternal spirit, the higher principle of his being

themes: how much more shall the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without spot to God
purge your conscience from dead works to serve the living God
the blood of Christ cleansing the conscience
the crux of the eternal Spirit

schools.summary: Christ's cleansing blood (v.14) qualifies him: 'and for this cause he is the mediator (mesites — go-between, reconciler) of the new testament (diatheke — covenant)' — Christ standing between God and man as mediator of the new covenant, securing its blessings; 'that by means of death (his own death), for the redemption (apolytrosis — ransoming, deliverance) of the transgressions that were under the first testament' — his death redeeming even the sins committed under the old covenant, his atonement reaching back to cover the transgressions the old sacrifices could not truly take away; 'they which are called (the elect, those summoned by the gospel) might receive the promise of eternal inheritance' — the end of his mediation being that the called obtain the promised everlasting inheritance (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he is the mediator of the new testament, that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament
they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance
Christ the mediator of the new covenant by means of death
his death redeeming past transgressions

schools.summary: Barnes, Ellicott (with Clarke and Calvin) hold diatheke should be rendered 'covenant' throughout (consistent with vv.15, 18, the OT berith, and the overwhelming NT usage): 'for where a covenant is, there must of necessity be brought in the death of the covenanter (or of the appointed victim).' A covenant with God was ratified by the death of a sacrificial victim (Genesis 15; Exodus 24; Jeremiah 34:18 'cut the calf in twain'); the 'death of the testator (diathemenos)' being the death of the victim slain to confirm the covenant. Barnes argues at length that diatheke never means 'will' elsewhere in Scripture or the LXX, that Christ made no will and had no property, and that the argument concerns covenant-ratification by sacrifice (vv.18-22), not a legal will. Clarke: 'we should endeavour to forget that testament and testator were ever introduced, as they totally change the apostle's meaning.' Ellicott: the difficulties of the 'will' reading are 'beyond comparison more serious.'

Calvin: the Greek diatheke includes both 'covenant' and 'testament,' and the apostle, alluding to its secondary meaning, dwells chiefly on the thing itself — that God's covenant, like a testament, was ratified by blood and death; the heathen ratified covenants by sacrifice (a corruption derived from God's institution), and the covenant of God made with blood may fitly be compared to a testament, being of the same kind. Gill follows the 'testament/will' reading (the covenant of grace a will bequeathing grace and glory); Robertson notes the word plays on both senses ('we say today The New Testament'), both terms pertinent; Wesley thinks it 'beneath the dignity of the apostle to play upon the ambiguity.'

sides.position: 'Testament/will' — a will takes effect at the maker's death (the AV reading); so Christ's death made his testament effective
'Covenant' — ratified by the death of the sacrificial victim/covenanter, consistent with vv.15, 18 and the OT berith

themes: For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator
the principle that a testament requires the death
the crux of covenant or testament
death necessary to ratify the covenant

schools.summary: The explanation of v.16: 'for a testament is of force (valid, ratified) after men are dead (epi nekrois — over the dead): otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth' — whether of a will (valid only at the maker's death) or a covenant (ratified over the dead sacrificial victim), it has no force until death intervenes. Barnes (with Clarke) holds 'over the dead' refers to the slain sacrificial victims that ratify the covenant (the covenant confirmed over dead sacrifices, not valid while the victim lives), noting that 'dead' (nekrois) need not mean only dead men but can include dead animals (cf. 'dead works,' 6:1; 9:14; a dead lion, Ecclesiastes 9:4); the AV/Gill reading takes it of a human will valid only after the maker dies. Either way, death is indispensable to validity, confirming Christ's death was necessary to ratify the new covenant.

themes: For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth
the testament takes force only by death
death indispensable to validity
confirming Christ's death was necessary

schools.summary: The application: 'whereupon (for which reason, in keeping with the principle that death ratifies) neither the first testament (covenant) was dedicated (inaugurated, ratified) without blood' — even the old covenant (the Sinai covenant) was not established without blood, confirmed with blood at its inauguration (Exodus 24); this historical fact (detailed in vv.19-21) proving that the principle of vv.16-17 (death ratifies a covenant) held even for the old covenant, and so applies to the new, confirming that Christ's death ratifies the new covenant (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood
the first covenant too was dedicated with blood
even the Sinai covenant ratified by blood
proving the principle that death ratifies

schools.summary: The historical account (Exodus 24): 'for when Moses had spoken every precept (commandment) to all the people according to the law, he took the blood of calves and of goats, with water, and scarlet wool, and hyssop, and sprinkled both the book (the book of the covenant), and all the people' — after declaring the law, Moses ratifying the covenant by taking the blood (the means of ratification), mixed and applied with the prescribed materials (the writer adding details — water, wool, hyssop, goats — drawn from the fuller priestly tradition), and sprinkling both the covenant document and the people, so consecrating both by blood and sealing the agreement (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people according to the law
he took the blood of calves and of goats... and sprinkled both the book, and all the people
Moses ratifying the old covenant by sprinkling blood
sealing the covenant with blood

schools.summary: Moses' solemn declaration (Exodus 24:8): 'saying, This is the blood of the testament (covenant) which God hath enjoined (commanded, ordained) unto you' — Moses, sprinkling the blood, pronouncing it the blood that ratified the covenant God had commanded, the blood by which the covenant was sealed and made binding, the people bound to God by it; the words strikingly echoed by Christ at the Last Supper ('This is my blood of the new testament,' Matthew 26:28), the old covenant's blood-ratification foreshadowing the new covenant sealed in Christ's blood (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you
Moses' words declaring the blood of the covenant
the blood sealing the covenant
echoed by Christ over the cup

schools.summary: Beyond the book and people (v.19), 'moreover he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry' — Moses (or Aaron) consecrating the sacred tent and all the implements of worship by blood, everything connected with the covenant worship sanctified by sprinkling; the tabernacle itself (the place of worship) purified and dedicated, and 'all the vessels of the ministry' (the altar, basins, and furniture) likewise sprinkled — the pervasiveness of blood in the old worship, everything cleansed and consecrated by it, proving its necessity (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Moreover he sprinkled with blood both the tabernacle, and all the vessels of the ministry
the tabernacle and vessels also sprinkled with blood
everything consecrated by blood
the pervasiveness of blood in the old worship

schools.summary: The summary principle: 'and almost (schedon — nearly, with few exceptions) all things are by the law purged (cleansed, purified) with blood' — under the law nearly everything cleansed by blood, the great means of purification (the 'almost' allowing for exceptions, e.g., cleansing by water or fire, Numbers 31:23, or the poor's offering of flour, Leviticus 5:11); 'and without shedding of blood (haimatekchysia) is no remission (aphesis — forgiveness, putting away of sin)' — the great maxim that there is no forgiveness of sin without the shedding of blood, blood (representing life given up in death) being the necessary price of pardon, the foundational principle of the whole sacrificial system, pointing to the necessity of Christ's death for true remission (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: if even the old covenant required blood for forgiveness, the true remission of sins required the shedding of Christ's blood; the principle 'no remission without bloodshed' finds its fulfilment in the cross, where the precious blood of Christ obtained the pardon the animal sacrifices could only foreshadow.

themes: almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission
the law's rule: almost all purged by blood
no remission without bloodshed
the necessity pointing to Christ's death

schools.summary: The argument from lesser to greater: 'it was therefore necessary that the patterns (hypodeigmata — copies, the earthly tabernacle and its furniture, copies of heavenly realities) of things in the heavens should be purified with these (the animal blood); but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these' — the earthly copies cleansed with animal sacrifices, but the heavenly realities requiring a better sacrifice (the blood of Christ); the question how 'heavenly things' could need 'purifying' answered (Barnes, Henry) not by supposing heaven defiled, but that the heavenly sanctuary is inaugurated and the way into it consecrated by Christ's blood, or that the people who enter heaven are purified — the 'purifying' being the consecration or opening of the heavenly things by a better sacrifice (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: the patterns of things in the heavens should be purified with these
the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these
the heavenly things purified with better sacrifices
the heavenly sanctuary consecrated by Christ's blood

schools.summary: The contrast: 'for Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands (the earthly tabernacle, built by men), which are the figures (antitypa — copies) of the true (the real, heavenly sanctuary); but into heaven itself' — Christ entering not the earthly Holy of Holies (a mere copy) but heaven itself, the true sanctuary, the very presence of God; 'now to appear (emphanisthenai — be manifested, present himself) in the presence of God (before God's face) for us (on our behalf)' — Christ now appearing before God for his people, presenting himself and his finished sacrifice, ever interceding, his entrance into heaven being for our sake (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: Christ entered heaven itself, not a copy, to appear before God's face for us — presenting his sacrifice and interceding on our behalf; his heavenly ministry is the reality the earthly high priest's yearly entrance only foreshadowed.

themes: Christ is not entered into the holy places made with hands, which are the figures of the true; but into heaven itself
now to appear in the presence of God for us
Christ entered heaven itself to appear before God for us
presenting his sacrifice, interceding

schools.summary: The difference: 'nor yet that he should offer himself often (repeatedly), as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others (allotrio — of another, the blood of animals, not his own)' — Christ not, like the Levitical high priest, offering repeatedly year by year; the high priest entering the Holy of Holies every year with fresh animal blood, a repeated, never-finished offering whose very repetition proved its insufficiency; whereas Christ offered his own blood, once — the double contrast (repeated vs once, others' blood vs his own) setting Christ's offering infinitely above the Levitical (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: nor yet that he should offer himself often, as the high priest entereth into the holy place every year with blood of others
Christ did not offer himself repeatedly like the high priest
once, not yearly; his own blood, not others'
the finality of his sacrifice

schools.summary: The reasoning against repetition: 'for then (if he had to offer repeatedly) must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world' — since Christ's offering was the offering of himself in death, repeated offering would require repeated suffering and dying, an absurdity since his death cannot be repeated; 'but now once (hapax — once for all) in the end of the world (the consummation of the ages, the close of the old order) hath he appeared (been manifested) to put away (athetesis — abolish, annul, do away with) sin by the sacrifice of himself' — Christ appearing a single time, at the climax of history, to abolish the guilt and power of sin by offering himself, his single self-sacrifice accomplishing the final putting-away of sin the repeated animal offerings never could (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: once, at the end of the ages, Christ appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself — a single, final abolition of sin, not to be repeated; his one self-offering accomplished what no repetition of animal blood could, the definitive removal of sin's guilt.

themes: then must he often have suffered since the foundation of the world
now once in the end of the world hath he appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself
Christ appearing once to put away sin
the final self-sacrifice

schools.summary: The universal appointment: 'and as it is appointed (apokeitai — set, ordained by God) unto men once to die' — death coming to every man once, the fixed, divine appointment and universal lot of mankind, the emphasis on 'once' (a single death, not repeated, against any notion of reincarnation) forming the parallel with Christ's single death (v.28); 'but after this the judgment' — death followed by judgment, the soul having died once coming to account before God, the certainty of judgment after death (against any idea of a second probation or escape) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment
men die once and then face judgment
the universal appointment of death
death followed by judgment

schools.summary: The correspondence with v.27: 'so (in like manner) Christ was once (once for all, answering to man's once dying) offered to bear (anenenkein — to carry, take up and away) the sins of many (the multitude of the redeemed, cf. Isaiah 53:12)' — as men die once, so Christ offered once, bearing the sins of many substitutionarily, taking on himself and carrying away his people's sins; 'and unto them that look for him (eagerly await him, the watching believers) shall he appear the second time without sin (choris hamartias — apart from sin, no longer bearing it) unto salvation' — as the high priest, having offered, came out to bless the waiting people, so Christ will appear a second time, not now to deal with sin (that finished) but to bring the full, final salvation (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ, having once borne the sins of many, will appear the second time 'without sin' — no longer as the sin-bearer but in glory, to bring salvation to those who look for him; his second coming is the believer's hope, the completion of the redemption his one offering secured.

themes: Christ was once offered to bear the sins of many
unto them that look for him shall he appear the second time without sin unto salvation
Christ once offered, appearing a second time for salvation
the one offering and the second coming
